scenario: Developer chooses between Docker Compose vs C8 Run for local dev. Wonders the trade-offs.
question: Docker Compose vs C8 Run for dev setup?

options.text: Both C8 Run and Docker Compose are official local development options for Camunda 8. Docker Compose provides a complete containerized stack including Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, Connectors, and Identity, giving developers a production-similar environment with full component control. C8 Run is an alternative bundled binary for quick starts. Claiming only C8 Run is valid ignores Docker Compose which is documented and recommended for teams needing production-aligned local setups.

Two valid options with different trade-offs: C8 Run is a bundled binary that starts a Camunda stack natively without Docker -- ideal for quick local exploration. Docker Compose runs the full containerized stack with Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, Connectors, and Identity -- better for production-similar development and team alignment. Choose C8 Run for fast onboarding; choose Docker Compose when component-level control, multi-developer consistency, or Connector testing is needed. Both are officially supported.

Local development environments for Camunda 8 are well-supported and officially documented. C8 Run provides a native installer for running a complete Camunda stack locally, and Docker Compose provides a containerized alternative. Both are featured in the official developer quickstart guide. Claiming that neither option enables local development is factually incorrect and contradicts the documented developer workflow that teams use to build and test processes before deploying to production clusters.

a.text: Incorrect. Local dev options for Camunda 8: C8 Run (Camunda 8 Run): What it is: Bundled installer with all Camunda 8 components. Pre-configured for dev use. Cross-platform: Windows, macOS, Linux. What's included: Zeebe (broker + gateway). Operate. Tasklist. Connectors. Web Modeler (some versions). Identity (some versions). Pros: Easy start: download + run. No Docker required: helpful if Docker unavailable / restricted. Sensible defaults: ready out of the box. Lightweight: smaller resource footprint than full container stack. Cons: Less customisable: hard to swap individual versions. Less production-like: prod is usually containerised. Port conflicts (per Set 13 Q60): may conflict with other apps. Use cases: First-time exploration. Quick proof-of-concept. Local development without infrastructure overhead. Docker Compose: What it is: Stack definition (docker-compose.yml) with all components as containers. Spin up entire Camunda 8 stack with one command. Setup: `bash git clone https://github.com/camunda/camunda-platform cd camunda-platform docker compose up -d ` Pros: Production-like: containers similar to prod deployment. Version control: pin specific versions of each component. Customisable: edit yaml to suit needs. Reproducible: same setup across team. Easy reset: tear down + recreate. Cons: Requires Docker: not always available. Resource-heavy: full stack uses significant RAM / CPU. More complex: yaml to understand + maintain. Startup time: slower than native binary. Use cases: Production-like dev. Team consistency. CI / automated testing. Customisations (e.g., custom Connectors). Decision matrix: | Need | Recommendation | |------|----------------| | Just trying things out | C8 Run | | Hate Docker | C8 Run | | Need prod-similar dev | Docker Compose | | Multiple devs need same setup | Docker Compose | | Custom Connectors / extensions | Docker Compose | | Limited resources | C8 Run | | CI testing | Docker Compose | Hybrid approaches: Pattern 1: C8 Run + selective container: C8 Run for core; add containers for extras. Custom mix. Pattern 2: Docker Compose with subset: Comment out unneeded services. Lighter Docker stack. Pattern 3: Camunda SaaS for dev: Use a SaaS cluster for dev (free trial / cheap tier). No local infra at all. Pros: real cloud; Cons: requires internet, may not be air-gapped. For onboarding: Document chosen approach: clear getting-started guide. Provide quick-start script: one-command setup. Troubleshoot common issues: port conflicts, etc. For long-running dev: Consider Docker even if C8 Run easier initially. Production parity pays off in fewer surprises. Other considerations: Network: Both expose default ports (8080, 26500, etc.). Configurable; useful for port conflict resolution. Persistence: C8 Run: state in local filesystem. Docker Compose: volumes; can persist or reset. Updates: C8 Run: download new version. Docker Compose: pull new images; recreate. Multi-instance: C8 Run: one instance per machine (port collisions). Docker Compose: can run multiple stacks with different ports. Best practices: Match dev to prod: closer the better. Reset frequently: clean state catches issues. Source-control config: yaml in Git for team alignment. Document quirks: ports, paths, etc. Anti-patterns: Dev only on SaaS: high cost; vendor lock-in. Inconsistent setups across team: works-on-my-machine bugs. No teardown: stale state accumulates. For the scenario (developer choosing): Pure exploration / first try: C8 Run. Real development: Docker Compose. Team alignment: Docker Compose. Custom stack: Docker Compose. Both officially supported; pick based on needs.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
